segments.text: Uh, good morning everybody.
Uh, we now move, um, to a hearing
of evidence with, um, statistics New Zealand.
Uh, so essentially this is under the, um, umbrella of our
briefing on accountability settings for outcomes for Maori.
And what we've been doing over the course of, um,
some months is just, um, yeah, uh, having hearings
with different, uh, agencies, organizations with respect to,
um, public sector agencies being held
to account when it comes to outcomes, um, with Maori.
So we have you today for half an hour.
Um, I'd like to welcome general manager, Simon
and, uh, of data operations and infrastructure.
Anna McDowell, general Manager of Strategic Engage,
strategic Engagement, Maori Partnerships and Outcomes,
and Sean, general Manager
of Statistical Delivery Population and Housing.
Would you like to give us a bit of an intro
and then we can have a bit of a, um, question
and answer our time?
Absolutely. Um, so Koto and us to be here today.
Um, so as I understand it, we are here to sort
of provide an overview of critical data needed
for the census and social investments.
Um, so in my area of stats, um, we look
after the integrated data infrastructure, the IDI, um,
more than happy to give a brief overview of that, if
that's useful before talking about
data for social investments. Very
Brief just for PIN for balance
As well. So, integrated data
infrastructure is a research database
that holds de-identified information
about people and households.
Um, and access to it is within the Stats n Secure Data Lab,
um, how the data in the IO is used.
It's used for research analysis
and evaluation by government departments, NGOs, academics
and research service providers.
And that's to inform decision making by ministers,
by government departments, NGOs,
and their understanding of value in their clients
and their communities and by other data users
and the evidence they produce.
And in terms of, there's lots of data
that comes into the IDI,
it comes from government departments, it comes from NGOs,
and then it's integrated together
so you can understand the kind of, um, health outcomes of,
um, well anybody.
And you can understand, educate as you outcomes of people
who perhaps, um, have poor health outcomes.
So it's the, the power of it is in the data
that's brought together from all the different sectors.
And, you know, because of this data, um,
and the sensitivity of it, we have, um, a couple
of key frameworks that keep it safe.
One is five safe, which is really the
rules about using the data.
And the other one is ing, which is the sort
of our ethics approach to evaluating research applications.
Thank you. Um, in terms of the, the importance
of the IDI for social investment
and the critical data, there's really three types of data
that are critical for social investment.
So demographic data, the characteristics of people,
particularly people who are, um, at risk of poor outcomes
data to identify needs.
So outcomes for those who are most at risk
and what they're likely to experience, and therefore where
and when to intervene to avoid poor outcomes.
And then the data about those service outcomes.
So the quality and effectiveness of services
and the outcomes for people who use those services.
So currently the IDI is stronger since coverage
of demographic data and data to identify need.
There's quite a lot of crossover
between the administrative data that we bring into the IDI
for research and the data that's needed, uh,
for social investment and the
data that's needed for the census.
Um, but Sean will talk in a minute about the data for census
in terms of bringing data into the IDI
for social investments.
Um, one of the things we've identified rather than
necessarily lots of new data sets, um, it's been important
to identify existing data sets
that would be more useful if they were updated more
frequently or if we had more recent data.
And so that's certainly a focus
for stats in bringing that data in.
One of the biggest gaps we have at the moment is data
about service outcomes.
And 'cause of that we're working closely
with the social investment agency
and went to puny, uh, to bring in data from NGOs
and from AU Water program.
So integrating more data about service services delivered
by NGOs helps us understand what happens to those cohorts
of people and then tracking
their outcomes through the other data.
The I, um, a few months ago,
minister Willis announced the creation
of the social investment fund part of this year's budget.
Um, so the Social Investment Agency anticipate up
to 20 outcomes based contracts being signed
and stats is prioritizing integration of data,
following those contracts into the IDI.
Um, and we're also taking in data following the new
contracting approach for Funnel Water, um,
which is also aligned to the social investment model.
And so there are, I think 169 funnel water providers
for commissioning agencies,
and we've signed data sharing agreements to bring
that data into the IDI.
Sure. Thank you.
Morning Koto.
Um, I thought I'd just touch on the census,
the new census model briefly to sort of set the scene.
Uh, in June, 2025, uh,
following cabinet endorsement Minister of Statistics
and the government statistician announced the, uh, details
of how the New Zealand census of population
and dwellings would be conducted in the future.
Uh, and the new approach is built in three main components,
uh, first one being administrative or admin data.
Um, and that's in a way where census data
and statistics will be produced, prioritizing the use
of those data sources.
Um, it's effectively the foundation
of your new census model,
and it reduces the need for everyone in the TU
to fill out a survey on Census day.
Um, the second component is a new annual sample survey
census attribute survey
that helps verify the accuracy of admin data.
It also collects additional information
that is misrepresented underrepresented
or not available in admin data.
So there's some communities
that don't show up in those data sources
that you still need surveys.
Uh, for the third component is tailored solutions.
Uh, it's effectively stats nz working with Iwi Maori
and priority communities to develop solutions
to help meet their data needs and aspirations.
So there your three basic components.
Um, I guess more specifically,
admin data is already doing a lot of the heavy lifting.
Um, it provides in some cases better coverage than surveys.
And when it comes to counting the population, not describing
but counting the population,
it is the best tool we have, right?
It is better than your traditional model, um,
of a survey of everyone in the country.
Um, we already have strong admin data for, uh, I guess a lot
of key census topics.
So some examples might be, uh, age birthplace,
how long someone has been an T or New Zealand, uh,
and income, if you're thinking about tax records,
benefit data and other other sources.
Um, and we c we are confident that we can produce quality
statistics, uh, in those types of topics
and areas using the admin data first approach.
Um, I think when it comes to admin data,
it's not about relying on one single source.
Uh, you're integrating data from multiple sources, uh,
for different census variables.
So if you take something like ethnicity, uh,
people show up in more than one data set
and nearly everyone shows up somewhere.
And we use statistical techniques and rules to combine
or integrate those data sources
and choose the most reliable value
or values for each person.
'cause you might identify
as more than one ethnicity, for example. Sorry
To interrupt you. Can you give
us a few examples of, um, those data sources?
You, you can have, um, so if you're thinking about income,
that could be your tax records from Inland Revenue, uh,
you could be thinking about, I guess your national health,
uh, index, um, data set from Ministry
of Health could be benefits data from Ministry
of Social Development.
Um, it could be enrollment data from the education sector.
It's quite, quite varied.
Um, and it's depending on different interactions with, uh,
public services over a lifetime.
Thank you. Um, we are actively, uh, improving the data
and we're exploring new admin data sources, uh, at present.
Um, the trick there though is
that it's a whole of government effort.
Uh, it's not something that Stats NZ can do, uh, alone.
Um, it requires a lot
of collaboration across
different agencies and Crown entities.
Uh, and in some cases you might need, uh,
I guess formal levers to help drive that work
and set clear expectations around, uh,
data standards and, and adoption.
Um, so we, I guess to that end, we've been working
with a core group of agencies to develop a list
of the most relevant and valuable data sources, uh,
for census variables.
And we're working towards, um,
developing an admin data supply plan effectively.
And that's setting out which variables need improvement
by way of their quality
and consistency of collection, uh,
where we might need new data supplies, uh,
and where that the plan also sets out which data sets which
variables and which agencies will supply them
and when the changes are needed
and the timeframes for delivering those improvements.
So they're not all gonna be instantaneous.
It's gonna be a gradual progression.
So the Minister of Statistics is intending to take that plan
to cabinet in early to mid 2026, um,
and it'll feature, uh, core census demographic variables.
And we haven't got all of the details nailed down yet.
You've got, I guess, live, um, you know, collaboration
and deliberations going on with different agencies,
but we expect to see things like usual residence address.
Um, so if you're thinking about that in the context
of university students
and still using their parents' home address, for example,
um, thinking about, uh, your most detailed ethnicity.
Um, so I guess, uh, think about that instead
of collecting it at a, say Pacific people's level at sort
of individual Pacific Island nation level, um, gender,
uh, and Maori descent as well.
I guess thinking about Maori descent, we are confident
that we can produce good quality statistics from admin data
sources on that topic,
but we know that there are gaps in coverage when you think
about Maori that are born overseas, in particular Australia,
and how you go about collecting that data, um,
through different mechanisms if it's a New Zealand traveler
decoration or other sources. I'll stop there.
Thank you. Uh, yes. Um, Gregory,
I would like To ask you Yeah, just can you just, um,
um, extrapolate on that last one a little bit,
just expand on, so we are looking to
include in the data set, um, people
who are not New Zealand residents,
So resident, they might,
they might not be New Zealand

but we'd only start, we'd only include them in the
data set once they're actually

Cool. So we know we've got really good coverage from,
let's say, when births are registered.
Yes. Um, and,
but we know there's a gap, um, for where we've got, um,
people of Mari descent who weren't born in New Zealand

Gotcha. Yeah. Okay, thanks.
So thank you Greg.

I was here more for questions, so yes,
there's absolutely nothing I need to add to that.
Um, just help out with questions

to know about other
collaborative approaches that we're taking.
Otherwise, happy just to, to,
um, work through your questions
And colleagues open to the floor.
Then Adrian,
Just on that topic, I just wanted to know, um, sort

Do you get down into Hapu or just Iwi level?

Uh, we need to work across, uh, government agencies

our I and Maori partners.

the capture of iwi affiliation
information and the new census
Approach Is, uh, just a supplementary on that, is, does

with those particular iwi sharing data?
'cause that some of them have pretty good data,

Yeah, so there is, um, yeah,
there's variability in the capture across the country.
So not only in in government but also with iwi themselves.
Um, we already work with a number of iwi on, um,
building the data capability that, that they have

we'll continue to do that work
and as we can make sure that that's relevant
for them in the census context as well.
So I think, um,
all those avenues are certainly being looked at

Thank you. That's okay. Um, any other,
Yeah, I was just wondering if you could give us an example

that the other options survey data, for example,
could potentially capture it.
'cause obviously there's a risk here that people might fall

Is it reasonable to expect we can capture in other ways,

I'll, I'll give a broad example.
Um, and I'll go back to I guess your most granular ethnicity

it is collected in different ways across government
departments and, and crown entities.
So we want to get to a point where you have consistency, um,

So that's your most granular level.
And then you also, part of

And so at present you might have, um,
that standard adopted in say, uh, an education sector.
Um, you might have a slightly different, uh, variation of

Um, and it could be, um, you know, it could be, uh,
you know, to, to a good quality and say MSD or

where data collection, uh, through those, I guess public,

And so are there areas where you feel
that won't be captured
or, sorry, I, I guess perhaps aside from that,

complete data across those in terms
of variability of how it's reported.
Or is it actually that there may be people

in any of these data sets?
'cause obviously that's a risk under the
current census model.
Is there form at all?
And we don't know they exist necessarily, so That's right.
Is it that risk of hey,
we're not capturing someone in their entirety,
or we're capturing lots of people
but only partial dataset? Yeah,
I guess that comes back to the point
that we're not reliant on a, a single admin data source.
Yes. Right. So you're integrating multiple sources to ensure

you can possibly achieve.
And then you can couple that with your survey data sources.
You can couple that with, uh, historical census data sets,
um, although they do lose value over time.
And then you've got statistical techniques to, um,
around imputation to help address your gaps as well.
So by the time you work through those different data sources
and those different statistical techniques, you end up
with a good quality coverage for these different, um, topics
and variables that come out of census.
Okay. Can I add a little bit? Yeah, of course.
Um, so I think the three component parts
of the new approach are really important too.
'cause your question is really valid.
So, and in the traditional census model, you know, we were,
we were already worried about groups in the population
that we weren't necessarily capturing as best
or that to the level that we know we need to.
Um, and I think that's, um,
really important in the new approach is we will still need

and that would will be where a lot of effort goes.
Um, and the three different component parts of the model,
so the admin data, the annual, uh, sample survey,

because we want to be able to work through that with

And, and so the three component
parts together will work together.
And I think the other really useful thing about this new

So we will have opportunities on an annual basis
or sort of over two to three years
where we are looking at different collections
or different ways in which we're capturing data,
different ways to partner to, um,
to make sure we've got that information.
So the opportunities are there, there will be challenges

but we are really looking forward to
how we can work differently
and more flexibly, um, to be able to address some of those,
those potential challenges.
Thank you. Pardon? 1 0 1, uh, a quick one.

we are collaborating with,

They're not all of those communities not, uh,

Um, and hence I guess working towards, uh, better adoption

Uh, we've got homelessness and transient, um, people.
Uh, so we, we, I guess when you're thinking about those

So in a, in a, I guess disability, uh, persons, uh, context,
we are working with FA Kaha on a, um,
disability data framework.

for people with disabilities.
Um, and that's basically to understand

of different aspects of those population

with interacting with society.
Another example might be in the homelessness

So we understand, um, that that population for,

you're saying, uh,
what is informing our tailored solutions?

'cause they might be different to, uh, policy problems, um,

and know that you're working with, uh,
those communities in iwi Maori over

I guess work towards addressing their unmet need.

source_metadata.title: 2025/11/05 - MA
source_metadata.description:
Wednesday 05 November 2025 | Māori Affairs Committee 

Briefing on accountability settings and outcomes for Māori

Hearing of evidence 
Submitter List:
Stats NZ Tatauranga Aotearoa

Simon Ross, General Manager – Data Operations and Infrastructure
Anna McDowell, General Manager – Strategic Engagement, Māori Partnerships and Outcomes
Sean Broughton, General Manager – Statistical Delivery, Population and Housing
